hero.lead: Before the first line goes on the wood: what is a pin, what is it good for, and which types are there? Understand first — then lay out.
hero.trust: Every answer from verified master knowledge

overviewQueries: Why does it hold without screws?

what.title: Two boards that interlock like the fingers of two hands.
what.body: At the end of each board — the end grain — a comb-like pattern is cut out. Push the boards together at the corner, and each pin fits into the gap in the other. In a dovetail the pins and tails are wedge-shaped: they lock together and hold even without glue or screws.
what.note: A cabinetmaker calls this a mechanical lock — the shape itself holds the corner together. That's why the dovetail is the strongest corner joint in furniture making.

history.title: Older than you'd think — and <hl>never obsolete</hl>.

angle.title: 1:6 or 1:8 — <hl>which angle?</hl>
angle.sub: The dovetail angle sets how wedge-shaped the pins and tails are. Pick one and see the difference:

ris.note: Anchored in law. The {aoTitel} (RIS — Austria's official legal information system, statute no. {gesNr}) mandates laying out and dovetail joints verbatim as required skills; the {lpTitel} cites {zitat}. This learning tool covers exactly one legally mandated core skill of the Austrian cabinetmaker apprenticeship.

ask.title: Ask, as if he's at the next bench.
ask.sub: Ask a question — the answer comes from the verified trade corpus, with its source, even with no network at all.

sampleQueries: Why does the tail formula use the number 1.7
Can you glue long grain to cross grain

anglePicker.svgLabel: Dovetail at a ratio of 1 to {ratio}, {deg}

6.title: 1:6 — the steeper angle
6.text: The tails wedge in harder — more mechanical hold. The tips are sturdier. The classic choice for coarser work.

8.title: 1:8 — the finer angle
8.text: Looks more elegant and delicate. Popular for fine furniture. An even steeper angle would leave short end grain at the tips, and that breaks out.

anglePicker.note: The difference: Both hold firm. 1:6 is steeper and locks harder, 1:8 is shallower and looks finer. Which ratio goes with which wood is a matter of workshop tradition — your master decides that.
